content.problemStatement:
Alice has an array of NNN integers — A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. She wants the product of all the elements of the array to be a non-negative integer. That is, it can be either 000 or positive. But she doesn't want it to be negative.

To do this, she is willing to remove some elements of the array. Determine the  minimum number of elements that she will have to remove to make the product of the array's elements non-negative.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of elements in the array originally.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers — A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, which are the original array elements.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of elements that she has to remove from the array.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of elements that must be removed so that the product of the remaining elements is non‑negative.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: No zero and an even number of negatives (0), product is positive → 0 removals.
Test case 2: No zero and one negative → product negative, remove the single negative → 1 removal.
Test case 3: Contains a zero, product is zero → 0 removals.
Test case 4: Two negatives (even), product positive → 0 removals.

hidden.label: general case 2 – even negatives, no zero

solution.approach:
For each test case count how many zeros and how many negative numbers are present.
* If there is at least one zero, the product of the whole array is already zero (non‑negative). No removal is needed.
* If there is no zero, the sign of the product depends only on the parity of the number of negative elements. If the count of negatives is even, the product is positive – answer 0.
* If the count of negatives is odd, the product is negative. Removing any single negative element makes the count even, turning the product positive. Hence the minimum removals required is 1.
The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the size of the array.
